refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits during agreed network conditions.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) supporting teleoperation as a capability, and (2) meeting a specific quality metric (packet loss). The refined FR isolates the quality/performance obligation while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing 'how' (e.g., redundancy, error correction, protocol selection) and focuses on the outcome: communication integrity under defined conditions. The phrase 'customer-defined packet loss limits' and 'agreed network conditions' preserves the acceptance criteria without embedding them as design constraints.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct teleoperation trials under agreed network conditions (e.g., 4G/5G coverage, latency profile, interference scenarios). Measure end-to-end packet loss across representative mission durations and worksite locations. Confirm measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined target (e.g., ≤1%). Document network conditions, test duration, packet count, and loss rate.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The NGHE solution shall enable remote operation of equipment from a tele-operator station.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation command and feedback data transmission with packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit under agreed network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement adaptive packet redundancy and forward error correction (FEC) with dynamic adjustment based on real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency jitter) to maintain communication integrity within customer-defined thresholds.

DP2: Establish a dual-channel communication architecture with independent logical paths and automatic failover switching to preserve teleoperation commands and feedback during transient packet loss events.

DP3: Deploy a buffering and retransmission protocol with acknowledgment-based flow control, tuned to customer network conditions, to recover lost packets within acceptable latency bounds for safe teleoperation.

DP4: Implement predictive network quality monitoring with preemptive operator notification and graceful degradation of teleoperation fidelity (reduced command frequency, simplified feedback) when packet loss approaches customer limits.

DP5: Combine selective packet prioritization (command > feedback > diagnostics) with congestion-aware scheduling to ensure critical teleoperation messages traverse within loss tolerance even under network stress.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP1 + DP5 (integrated): Implement adaptive packet redundancy and forward error correction with dynamic tuning based on real-time network quality metrics, combined with selective packet prioritization and congestion-aware scheduling. This ensures critical teleoperation commands and essential feedback maintain integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits (≤1%) across agreed network conditions, while non-critical diagnostic traffic is deprioritized under congestion.

potential_sources: Coupling between network quality monitoring (DP1 metric collection) and adaptive FEC/redundancy adjustment—mitigated by decoupling monitoring logic from adjustment logic via a well-defined quality metric interface.
Coupling between packet prioritization (DP5) and retransmission protocol—mitigated by ensuring prioritization operates at the scheduler layer independent of lower-layer FEC mechanisms.
Coupling to customer network conditions—explicitly parameterized (not hardcoded), allowing independent tuning per deployment without design rework.
coupling_check.notes: The integrated DP avoids the tight coupling of DP2 (dual-channel failover, which introduces redundant hardware/logical paths and complicates synchronization) and DP3 (pure retransmission, which may exceed latency budgets in high-loss scenarios). DP1+DP5 separates concerns: FEC/redundancy handles random loss; prioritization handles congestion-induced loss. Both are independently tunable and do not require architectural duplication.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss limit (≤1%) is achievable within the agreed network conditions (e.g., WiFi, LTE, satellite link) without requiring 100% redundancy or zero-latency guarantees.

ASSUMPTION: Real-time network quality metrics (loss rate, latency jitter) can be reliably estimated from the communication channel itself (e.g., via ACK patterns, probe packets) without external instrumentation.

RISK: If network conditions degrade beyond the design envelope (e.g., loss > 10%, latency > 500ms), adaptive FEC may saturate bandwidth, causing feedback starvation and loss of teleoperation situational awareness. Mitigation: Operator notification and graceful degradation (DP4 concept) should be integrated.

RISK: Packet prioritization may starve non-critical diagnostics indefinitely under sustained congestion, reducing observability for fault diagnosis. Mitigation: Implement minimum bandwidth guarantee for diagnostic traffic or periodic diagnostic sampling.

RISK: FEC overhead (redundant packets) increases bandwidth consumption; if customer network has strict throughput limits, FEC tuning may conflict with command/feedback fidelity requirements. Mitigation: Parameterize FEC redundancy ratio and validate against customer bandwidth budget.

ASSUMPTION: Teleoperation latency budget (not explicitly stated in FR) is compatible with retransmission delays introduced by adaptive mechanisms. If latency is critical (e.g., <100ms round-trip), FEC must be tuned conservatively.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: FR (refined): The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication integrity by ensuring that critical teleoperation commands and essential feedback packets experience packet loss ≤ [customer-defined target, default 1%] under agreed network conditions, with a maximum acceptable round-trip latency of [customer-defined, e.g., 200ms] for safe operator control. The solution shall employ adaptive forward error correction and packet prioritization, tuned to customer network characteristics, and shall notify the operator and gracefully degrade teleoperation fidelity if network conditions exceed design limits. Non-critical diagnostic traffic may be deprioritized or sampled during congestion.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: Encapsulates adaptive packet redundancy, forward error correction with dynamic tuning, selective packet prioritization, and congestion-aware scheduling for teleoperation command and feedback streams. Owns the real-time network quality assessment logic and packet handling policies that ensure critical teleoperation traffic meets customer-defined packet loss limits (≤1%) under agreed network conditions.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for transmitting/receiving packets with adaptive redundancy and FEC encoding; receives real-time network quality metrics (latency, loss, jitter) to inform dynamic tuning.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operator commands and teleoperation directives; Teleoperation module prioritizes these as critical traffic requiring highest integrity guarantees.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Outputs vehicle feedback and sensor data; Teleoperation module selectively deprioritizes non-critical diagnostic telemetry under congestion while preserving essential feedback for safe operation.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Signals safety-critical events that may trigger congestion-aware scheduling adjustments; ensures safety alerts are never deprioritized regardless of network congestion state.

module_containment_scores.notes: Excellent fit. Teleoperation is the natural owner of command/feedback integrity mechanisms. Adaptive redundancy, FEC, and prioritization are intrinsic to teleoperation quality-of-service. Real-time network quality metrics flow naturally from Network Link. Minor diffusion: safety-critical deprioritization logic may require coordination with Safety Services for override semantics, but primary ownership remains Teleoperation.

Moderate fit but suboptimal. While safety-critical packet handling (e.g., ensuring SafetyAlert is never deprioritized) is relevant, Safety Services should not own the general packet redundancy and FEC tuning logic—that is fundamentally a teleoperation QoS concern. Placing the entire DP here would diffuse teleoperation-specific scheduling and prioritization policies away from their natural domain. Better as a policy consumer than owner.

Poor fit. Vehicle module is responsible for vehicle state, actuation, and sensor integration—not network protocol handling. Placing adaptive redundancy, FEC, and congestion-aware scheduling in Vehicle would create tight coupling between vehicle dynamics and network transport logic, violating separation of concerns. High diffusion of network QoS concerns across vehicle subsystems.

Weak fit. NGHE Solution is likely a top-level system container. While it could theoretically host Teleoperation as a sub-module, assigning the DP to NGHE Solution directly would be overly broad and would obscure ownership boundaries. Better to assign to the specific Teleoperation module within NGHE Solution.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the most specific and cohesive boundary for the integrated DP. It naturally owns the logic for ensuring command and feedback integrity under network constraints because (1) teleoperation commands and essential feedback are the primary traffic streams requiring guaranteed packet loss limits, (2) adaptive redundancy and FEC tuning are teleoperation QoS mechanisms, (3) selective prioritization of teleoperation vs. diagnostic traffic is a teleoperation scheduling policy, and (4) real-time network quality metrics inform teleoperation-specific adaptation. External interfaces (Network Link for transport, Tasking for commands, Telemetry for feedback, SafetyAlert for safety override) are all well-defined and minimize diffusion.
containment_risks: Safety-critical packet handling (e.g., ensuring SafetyAlert is never deprioritized) may require explicit coordination with Safety Services. Risk: Teleoperation module may not have authority to override its own prioritization for safety events. Mitigation: Define a clear SafetyAlert interface that Teleoperation respects as highest priority, with Safety Services owning the policy of when SafetyAlert is raised.

Network Link interface must provide real-time quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) with sufficient fidelity and latency for dynamic tuning. Risk: If Network Link cannot supply metrics fast enough, Teleoperation's adaptive logic will be ineffective. Mitigation: Establish SLA on Network Link metric delivery (e.g., <100ms latency, <1s update interval).

Congestion-aware scheduling logic may conflict with other system priorities (e.g., vehicle safety loops, diagnostics). Risk: Deprioritizing diagnostic traffic could mask critical vehicle faults. Mitigation: Define a whitelist of traffic types that are never deprioritized, coordinated with Vehicle and Safety Services.

Customer-defined packet loss limits (≤1%) and agreed network conditions must be configurable and validated at deployment. Risk: If limits are set unrealistically for actual network conditions, Teleoperation cannot guarantee compliance. Mitigation: Teleoperation module should validate configuration against Network Link capabilities and raise alerts if limits are unachievable.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: No revision to DP choice is necessary. The DP is well-scoped and logically sound. However, to improve containment clarity, recommend explicitly decomposing the DP into two sub-DPs within Teleoperation: (1) DP1-sub: Adaptive packet redundancy and FEC with dynamic tuning based on real-time network quality metrics (owned by Teleoperation's transport layer), and (2) DP5-sub: Selective packet prioritization and congestion-aware scheduling with safety-critical override semantics (owned by Teleoperation's scheduling layer, with explicit SafetyAlert interface to Safety Services). This decomposition makes ownership and interface contracts more explicit without changing the module boundary.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: High. Packet loss ≤1% is directly measurable via network telemetry and packet counters on the Network Link interface. Real-time network quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) flow from Network Link to Teleoperation module, enabling continuous compliance monitoring. Acceptance criteria are quantitative and testable under agreed network conditions (e.g., simulated packet loss scenarios, congestion injection tests). Teleoperation module can log adaptive tuning decisions and FEC/redundancy application rates for post-test analysis.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Excellent. The Teleoperation module is the clear and specific owner of the integrated DP. FR requirement (maintain teleoperation communication integrity within customer-defined packet loss limits) maps directly to Teleoperation's scope: adaptive redundancy, FEC, prioritization, and congestion-aware scheduling are all intrinsic to teleoperation QoS. External interfaces (Network Link, Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert) are well-defined and minimize ambiguity about responsibility boundaries. No allocation scatter detected.

diffusion_risks: Safety-critical packet handling (SafetyAlert deprioritization override) creates a soft dependency on Safety Services. Risk: Teleoperation module may lack authority to enforce safety-critical override semantics if Safety Services does not explicitly signal when SafetyAlert is raised. Mitigation: Define a formal SafetyAlert interface contract specifying that Teleoperation must treat SafetyAlert as highest-priority traffic regardless of congestion state, with Safety Services owning the policy of when SafetyAlert is triggered.

Network Link interface must deliver real-time quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) with sufficient fidelity and low latency (<100ms) for dynamic tuning to be effective. Risk: If Network Link metric delivery is delayed or coarse-grained, Teleoperation's adaptive logic will lag behind actual network conditions, reducing compliance with packet loss limits. Mitigation: Establish explicit SLA on Network Link metric delivery (e.g., <100ms latency, <1s update interval) and validate at integration.

Congestion-aware scheduling logic may inadvertently deprioritize diagnostic traffic that masks critical vehicle faults. Risk: Non-critical diagnostic telemetry deprioritization could hide safety-relevant sensor anomalies. Mitigation: Define a whitelist of traffic types (e.g., vehicle health, fault codes) that are never deprioritized, coordinated with Vehicle and Safety Services modules.

Customer-defined packet loss limits (≤1%) and agreed network conditions are configuration inputs to Teleoperation. Risk: If limits are set unrealistically for actual network conditions, Teleoperation cannot guarantee compliance and will fail acceptance testing. Mitigation: Teleoperation module should validate configuration at startup and raise alerts if limits are unachievable given Network Link capabilities.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Containment score 0.92 is justified. Teleoperation is the natural and cohesive owner of the integrated DP. Adaptive redundancy, FEC, and prioritization are teleoperation-specific QoS mechanisms; they do not belong in Vehicle (which owns actuation/sensing), Safety Services (which owns safety policy, not transport QoS), or NGHE Solution (which is too broad). The 0.08 diffusion score reflects only the minor coordination required with Safety Services for SafetyAlert override semantics—a manageable interface contract, not a fundamental ownership problem. Recommended sub-decomposition (DP1-sub for transport layer, DP5-sub for scheduling layer) would further clarify internal Teleoperation structure but is not required for gate passage.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP is logically sound and directly addresses the FR. The integrated DP (DP1 + DP5) combines adaptive packet redundancy and FEC with selective prioritization and congestion-aware scheduling—a coherent solution for maintaining teleoperation integrity under packet loss. The DP explicitly references customer-defined packet loss limits (≤1%) and agreed network conditions, matching the FR's acceptance criteria. Dynamic tuning based on real-time network quality metrics ensures the solution adapts to actual network behavior rather than relying on static configuration. No logical gaps or contradictions detected. DP is implementable and testable.

recommended_revisions: Decompose the integrated DP into two explicit sub-DPs within Teleoperation to improve clarity: (1) DP1-sub: Adaptive packet redundancy and FEC with dynamic tuning based on real-time network quality metrics (transport layer), and (2) DP5-sub: Selective packet prioritization and congestion-aware scheduling with safety-critical override semantics (scheduling layer). This makes internal module structure and interface contracts more explicit without changing the Teleoperation module boundary.

Establish and document formal interface contracts: (a) Network Link SLA: real-time quality metrics delivery <100ms latency, <1s update interval; (b) SafetyAlert contract: Teleoperation must treat SafetyAlert as highest-priority traffic regardless of congestion, with Safety Services owning the policy of when SafetyAlert is raised; (c) Configuration validation: Teleoperation module validates customer-defined packet loss limits and agreed network conditions at startup and raises alerts if limits are unachievable.

Define a whitelist of traffic types (e.g., vehicle health, fault codes, safety alerts) that are never deprioritized under congestion, coordinated with Vehicle and Safety Services modules. Document this policy in Teleoperation module design and validate during integration testing.

Add acceptance test cases for dynamic tuning effectiveness: (a) Simulate varying packet loss (0–5%) and measure Teleoperation's adaptive response (redundancy/FEC adjustment); (b) Inject congestion and verify selective deprioritization of non-critical telemetry while preserving critical feedback and SafetyAlert; (c) Validate compliance with ≤1% packet loss target under agreed network conditions.

decision.justification: All three gates (FR-IM, DP-IM, FR-DP) pass with high confidence. The integrated DP is logically sound, allocation is clear, and verification is feasible. However, the recommended revisions—particularly decomposition into two explicit sub-DPs (transport layer and scheduling layer) and formalization of interface contracts—are not optional refinements; they are essential for managing the identified diffusion risks in a safety-critical context. The 'soft dependency' on Safety Services for SafetyAlert override semantics and the risk of inadvertent deprioritization of safety-relevant diagnostic traffic require explicit sub-level decomposition to establish clear ownership of transport vs. scheduling concerns and to make interface contracts (SafetyAlert priority, traffic whitelisting, Network Link SLA) analyzable at the next level. Given high safety criticality, proceeding to sub-decomposition is justified to reduce integration risk and ensure that safety-critical override semantics are not left implicit.
next_level_sub_frs: FR1-sub: Maintain adaptive packet redundancy and forward error correction (FEC) tuning based on real-time network quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) to minimize packet loss impact on teleoperation feedback.

FR5-sub: Enforce selective packet prioritization and congestion-aware scheduling with safety-critical override semantics to preserve critical teleoperation feedback and safety alerts under network congestion.

revise.why: Decompose the integrated DP into two explicit sub-DPs to clarify internal module structure and interface contracts: (1) DP1-sub: Adaptive packet redundancy and FEC with dynamic tuning based on real-time network quality metrics (transport layer); (2) DP5-sub: Selective packet prioritization and congestion-aware scheduling with safety-critical override semantics (scheduling layer). This decomposition makes the boundary between transport-layer QoS and scheduling-layer policy explicit, reducing ambiguity about which sub-module owns SafetyAlert override logic and traffic whitelisting. Formalize interface contracts at sub-level: Network Link SLA (<100ms metric latency, <1s update interval), SafetyAlert contract (Teleoperation treats SafetyAlert as highest-priority regardless of congestion, with Safety Services owning trigger policy), and traffic whitelist (vehicle health, fault codes, safety alerts never deprioritized).

assumptions: Network Link module can deliver real-time quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) with <100ms latency and <1s update interval; if not, adaptive tuning effectiveness is compromised.
Safety Services module will explicitly signal SafetyAlert state to Teleoperation; Teleoperation does not infer safety state from other signals.
Customer-defined packet loss limits (≤1%) are achievable given actual network conditions; Teleoperation can validate this at startup and raise alerts if limits are unrealistic.
Vehicle and Safety Services modules will coordinate with Teleoperation to define the whitelist of traffic types (vehicle health, fault codes, safety alerts) that are never deprioritized.
Congestion-aware scheduling logic will not inadvertently mask safety-relevant sensor anomalies by deprioritizing diagnostic telemetry that carries critical fault codes.
open_risks: SafetyAlert override semantics: If Safety Services does not explicitly signal SafetyAlert state or if Teleoperation lacks authority to enforce highest-priority treatment, safety-critical packets may be deprioritized under congestion. Mitigation: Formalize SafetyAlert interface contract at sub-level with explicit signal semantics and priority enforcement rules.

Network Link metric delivery SLA: If Network Link cannot deliver quality metrics with <100ms latency and <1s update interval, Teleoperation's adaptive tuning will lag behind actual network conditions, reducing compliance with ≤1% packet loss target. Mitigation: Establish and validate Network Link SLA during integration; add acceptance test cases for metric delivery latency.

Diagnostic telemetry deprioritization masking safety faults: Congestion-aware scheduling may inadvertently hide safety-relevant sensor anomalies by deprioritizing non-critical diagnostic traffic. Mitigation: Define and enforce a whitelist of traffic types (vehicle health, fault codes, safety alerts) that are never deprioritized; validate during integration testing.

Configuration validation: If customer-defined packet loss limits are set unrealistically for actual network conditions, Teleoperation cannot guarantee compliance. Mitigation: Teleoperation module validates configuration at startup and raises alerts if limits are unachievable given Network Link capabilities.

Sub-level interface clarity: Without explicit sub-DP decomposition, the boundary between transport-layer QoS (adaptive redundancy/FEC) and scheduling-layer policy (prioritization/override semantics) may remain ambiguous, increasing integration risk. Mitigation: Decompose into DP1-sub and DP5-sub with explicit interface contracts.
evidence_needed: Network Link SLA specification: Confirm that Network Link can deliver real-time quality metrics (loss, latency, jitter) with <100ms latency and <1s update interval; validate with integration testing.

SafetyAlert interface contract: Formalize the contract between Safety Services and Teleoperation specifying SafetyAlert signal semantics, priority enforcement rules, and conditions under which SafetyAlert is raised.

Traffic whitelist definition: Coordinate with Vehicle and Safety Services modules to define the whitelist of traffic types (vehicle health, fault codes, safety alerts) that are never deprioritized; document in Teleoperation module design.

Configuration validation logic: Implement and test Teleoperation's startup validation of customer-defined packet loss limits against Network Link capabilities; verify that alerts are raised if limits are unachievable.

Acceptance test cases: (a) Simulate varying packet loss (0–5%) and measure Teleoperation's adaptive response (redundancy/FEC adjustment); (b) Inject congestion and verify selective deprioritization of non-critical telemetry while preserving critical feedback and SafetyAlert; (c) Validate compliance with ≤1% packet loss target under agreed network conditions; (d) Verify that diagnostic telemetry whitelisted as safety-relevant is never deprioritized.

Sub-level DP decomposition: Develop explicit DP1-sub (transport layer) and DP5-sub (scheduling layer) with clear ownership, interface contracts, and internal structure; validate that decomposition reduces ambiguity and integration risk.
